rationale: This page contains detailed geographical and geopolitical information regarding the Taiwan Strait, the Red Sea, and Tiexian Reef. It includes specific details on bordering nations, strategic maritime connections, and territorial disputes in the South China Sea, which are highly relevant for current affairs examinations.
cleanedText:
Taiwan Strait
• Median Line or Davis Line: Runs approximately down the middle of the Taiwan Strait. (Not recognised by China).
• Geographical Context: The strait separates mainland China from Taiwan and connects the East China Sea with the South China Sea.
Red Sea
• News: Red Sea cargo ships face new attacks as Houthis (Yemen) claim to have sunk vessel.
• Description: Red Sea is a marginal sea in the north-west of the Indian Ocean.
• Connectivity: It is connected to the Gulf of Aden via Strait of Bab El Mandeb and to the Mediterranean sea via Suez Canal.
• Bordering Nations:
- West: Egypt, Sudan and Eritrea.
- North East: Israel & Jordan via the gulf of Aqaba.
- East: Saudi Arabia & Yemen.
• Northern Split: In its northern part, the Red Sea splits into two parts: the Gulf of Suez (Northwest) and Gulf of Aqaba (Northeast).
• Salinity: It contains one of the world’s most saline seawater.
- The Red Sea receives very little precipitation in any form, and no water enters the Red Sea from rivers.
Tiexian Reef
• News: China seizes disputed Tiexian Reef near a key Philippine military outpost of Thitu Island, also known as Pag-asa Island in the South China Sea.
• About Tiexian Reef:
- Alternate name: Sandy Cay; it is part of the Spratly Islands.
- Significance: China claims Sandy Cay is a natural feature and not man-made, entitling it to a 12-nautical-mile (22-km) territorial sea under international law that would overlap with Thitu Island.
- A territorial sea claim around Sandy Cay would provide a boost for nearby Subi Reef, one of China’s reclaimed and militarized deep-water ports.
• Regional Context: The area includes Thitu Island, Subi Reef, Whitsun Reef, and falls within the vicinity of China's "nine-dash" line claim in the South China Sea.
